text: Here we present a case of transanal total mesorectal excision using near infrared light to assist in the join up between the abdominal and transanal components.
The patient was a thirty seven year old male referred from a secondary centre with a low rectal cancer about one to one point five centimetres above the anal rectal junction.
MRI staging staged the tumour at T-to-N-zero, and the patient didn't have any neoadjuvant treatment.
The abdominal team uses a standard endo-eye laparoscope to continue the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, with the colon already mobilized.
The transanal team uses a fluorescence-enabled pinpoint laparoscope to dissect cranially past the pelvic floor, revealing the anatomical structures in the pelvic region.
The near-infrared mode is activated, revealing a signal in the anterior plane of dissection during the transanal total mesorectal excision.
Standard lap scopes emit light in the near-infrared spectrum as they only have filters blocking ultraviolet light emission.
The surgical field shows the area where the near-infrared signal is visible, with no white light present in this region.
This is because near-infrared light penetrates through tissue much easier than white light.
The abdominal team removes their light source from the abdomen while the near-infrared mode remains activated, allowing visualization of the surgical field without standard illumination.
The abdominal light source is reintroduced into the surgical field, revealing a near-infrared light signal on the pinpoint display, highlighting the area of interest in the plane of dissection.
The near-infrared light signal intensifies as the abdominal and transanal teams approach the junction point during the rectal cancer procedure.
